canto_title: The Entrance into the Fire and the Ascent to the Earthly Paradise

scenes.summary: Dante uses a metaphor of the setting sun and flowing rivers to describe the appearance of the Angel of God.
An Angel stands outside the flames, singing and instructing the holy souls to enter the fire.
Upon hearing the command, Dante is struck by intense fear, imagining human bodies being consumed by fire.
Virgil comforts Dante, explaining that the fire causes no harm and encouraging him to enter without fear.
Dante remains frozen by his conscience until Virgil reminds him that the fire stands between him and Beatrice.
Using the metaphor of Pyramus and Thisbe, Dante describes how his hard heart finally yields to Virgil's words.
Virgil is surprised by Dante's sudden change, and the group enters the flames with Stazio following behind.
Dante describes the overwhelming heat of the fire and finds comfort in Virgil's mentions of Beatrice.
A divine voice calls to the pilgrims from within a light, urging them to continue their ascent before night falls.
The group climbs a steep path through the rock, following the fading light of the sun.
As night approaches and the horizon darkens, the travelers stop to rest on the mountain slope.
Dante compares their state to sheep and shepherds resting quietly under the watch of a master.
Despite the darkness, Dante observes the bright stars before being overcome by a heavy sleep.
Dante dreams of a beautiful woman named Lia wandering a meadow and weaving a garland of flowers.
As the morning light breaks and the darkness retreats, Dante wakes to find his companions already rising.
Virgil speaks tenderly to Dante, promising that the day will bring satisfaction to his hunger and fatigue.
Dante feels an overwhelming, instinctive urge to climb higher toward the summit.
Upon reaching the top of the stairs, Virgil informs Dante that he has reached the limit of his ability to lead.
Virgil points out the sun, flowers, and greenery, inviting Dante to explore the Earthly Paradise freely.
Virgil formally relinquishes control, declaring that Dante's own judgment and free will are now supreme.
